edges.description: Spectrin, protein 4.1R, and related membrane-skeleton defects weaken the horizontal cytoskeletal network underlying the erythrocyte membrane.
Horizontal cytoskeletal weakness reduces mechanical stability and red-cell deformability.
Reduced membrane stability contributes to elliptocytic and poikilocytic red-cell morphology.
edges.target: Elliptocytic and Poikilocytic Red Cell Morphology

edges.description: Decreased deformability and stability shorten erythrocyte survival and produce hemolysis in symptomatic cases.
edges.target: Shortened Red Cell Survival and Hemolysis
Shortened Red Cell Survival and Hemolysis

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-phenotype correlations in hereditary elliptocytosis and hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.

nodes.description: Destabilized red-cell membrane architecture produces elliptical erythrocytes and, in more severe forms, poikilocytosis and red-cell fragmentation on peripheral smear.
nodes.id: Elliptocytic and Poikilocytic Red Cell Morphology

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-phenotype correlations in hereditary elliptocytosis and hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.
evidence.snippet: The clinical diagnosis of HE and HPP relies on identifying abnormal RBC morphology on peripheral blood smear (elliptocytes, poikilocytosis and fragmented RBCs)

evidence.reference_title: Molecular genetics of hereditary elliptocytosis and hereditary spherocytosis.
evidence.snippet: HE stems from changes in the SPTA1, SPTB, EL1 and (exceptionally) GPYC genes that encode spectrin alpha- and beta- chains, protein 4.1 and glycophorin C/D, respectively.

nodes.description: Variable hemolytic anemia may occur, from mild compensated hemolysis to severe transfusion-dependent disease in severe or biallelic presentations.

nodes.description: Red-cell transfusion is supportive therapy for severe congenital or transfusion-dependent hemolytic anemia in the severe HE/HPP spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary elliptocytosis (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: manifestations ranging from mild to severe transfusion-dependent hemolytic anemia

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-phenotype correlations in hereditary elliptocytosis and hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.
evidence.snippet: HPP is typically diagnosed in patients with family history of HE but presents with increased severity, frequently with transfusion requirement in infancy or, rarely, long-term transfusion-dependence.

nodes.description: Pathogenic variants in erythrocyte membrane-skeleton genes affect spectrin, protein 4.1R, or glycophorin C components that maintain red cell membrane shape and mechanical resilience.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary elliptocytosis: clinical, morphological and biochemical studies of 38 cases.
evidence.snippet: The major determinant of membrane shape and stability is a proteinaceous meshwork named membrane skeleton, composed mainly of spectrin, actin, protein 4.1 and ankyrin.

nodes.description: Defective cytoskeletal mechanics decrease erythrocyte deformability and mechanical stability, reducing red-cell survival and producing abnormal osmotic-gradient ektacytometry profiles.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary elliptocytosis: clinical, morphological and biochemical studies of 38 cases.
evidence.snippet: A study of red cell deformability using an ektacytometer revealed that the cell deformability under isotonic conditions was decreased in all HE patients.

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-phenotype correlations in hereditary elliptocytosis and hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.
evidence.snippet: The typical HE ektacytometry curve is trapezoidal with decreased RBC deformability

evidence.snippet: one in SPTB, one was compound heterozygous for mutations affecting both α- and β-spectrin and one had a mutation in EPB41.

nodes.description: Reduced membrane stability and deformability shorten erythrocyte survival. This drives variable hemolysis, ranging from compensated or asymptomatic disease to severe transfusion-dependent anemia.
nodes.id: Shortened Red Cell Survival and Hemolysis

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-phenotype correlations in hereditary elliptocytosis and hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.
evidence.snippet: The resulting defects alter the horizontal cytoskeletal associations and affect RBC membrane stability and deformability causing shortened RBC survival.

evidence.reference_title: Inherited hemolytic anemia: a possessive beginner's guide.
evidence.snippet: Membrane structural defects lead to hereditary spherocytosis (HS) and hereditary elliptocytosis (HE)

nodes.description: Splenectomy can ameliorate anemia in selected symptomatic patients with red-cell membrane structural disorders by increasing circulating red-cell lifespan; patient selection must consider severity and long-term risks.

evidence.reference_title: Inherited hemolytic anemia: a possessive beginner's guide.
evidence.snippet: splenectomy leads to amelioration of anemia by increasing the circulatory red cell life span.

nodes.description: Mutations affecting spectrin self-association or protein 4.1R-mediated junctional complexes weaken horizontal links in the erythrocyte cytoskeleton.

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-phenotype correlations in hereditary elliptocytosis and hereditary pyropoikilocytosis.
evidence.snippet: The resulting defects alter the horizontal cytoskeletal associations and affect RBC membrane stability and deformability causing shortened RBC survival.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary elliptocytosis: clinical, morphological and biochemical studies of 38 cases.
evidence.snippet: We observed two types of membrane defects in the 38 patients studied: 24 patients (13 kindreds) exhibited spectrin self-association defect (type I HE) and 14 patients (6 kindreds) displayed deficiency in protein 4.1.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary pyropoikilocytosis and elliptocytosis in a Caucasian family. Transmission of the same molecular defect in spectrin through three generations with different clinical expression.
evidence.snippet: Our studies revealed a defective ability of spectrin to self-associate
